title: Building Accessible Linux Applications: AT-SPI2 and Screen Reader Support
subtitle: Make your .NET MAUI Linux applications inclusive with comprehensive accessibility features, from screen readers to keyboard navigation.
pullQuotes: Accessibility isn't a feature—it's a fundamental requirement that ensures your application can be used by everyone, regardless of their abilities.
The IAccessible interface serves as the bridge between your MAUI controls and the Linux accessibility ecosystem, translating UI state into information that assistive technologies can understand.
Testing with Orca isn't just about compliance—it's about experiencing your application the way thousands of users will interact with it every day.

sections.content:
When building desktop applications for Linux, accessibility is not just a nice-to-have feature—it's essential for creating inclusive software that serves all users. The OpenMaui.Platform.Linux project demonstrates how .NET MAUI applications can achieve full accessibility compliance on Linux through the **AT-SPI2** (Assistive Technology Service Provider Interface) framework.

Linux desktop environments rely on AT-SPI2 to provide a standardized accessibility layer that screen readers like Orca, magnifiers, and other assistive technologies use to interact with applications. For developers building cross-platform .NET MAUI applications, understanding how to integrate with AT-SPI2 ensures that your Linux users—including those with visual, motor, or cognitive disabilities—can fully access your application's functionality.

This article explores the practical implementation of accessibility features in the OpenMaui Linux platform, covering everything from the foundational `IAccessible` interface to testing your application with the Orca screen reader. Whether you're porting an existing MAUI application to Linux or building a new one from scratch, these patterns will help you create applications that are accessible by design.

**Why Accessibility Matters:**
- **Legal compliance**: Many jurisdictions require digital accessibility
- **Market reach**: Approximately 15% of the global population has some form of disability
- **Better UX for everyone**: Accessible design benefits all users, not just those with disabilities
- **Technical excellence**: Proper accessibility implementation often reveals and fixes underlying architectural issues

AT-SPI2 (Assistive Technology Service Provider Interface, version 2) is the de facto standard for accessibility on Linux desktop environments. It provides a D-Bus-based protocol that allows assistive technologies to query and manipulate application UI elements programmatically.

## How AT-SPI2 Works

AT-SPI2 operates on a client-server model:

1. **Application (Server)**: Your application exposes its UI hierarchy through the AT-SPI2 protocol
2. **Assistive Technology (Client)**: Screen readers like Orca, magnifiers, or voice control systems connect to your application via D-Bus
3. **Registry**: The `at-spi2-registryd` daemon manages connections between applications and assistive technologies

## Key Concepts

**Accessible Objects**: Every UI element in your application that should be accessible must be represented as an accessible object with:
- **Role**: What the element is (button, text field, label, etc.)
- **State**: Current condition (focused, checked, enabled, visible, etc.)
- **Properties**: Name, description, value, and other metadata
- **Actions**: Operations that can be performed (click, focus, toggle, etc.)
- **Relations**: Connections to other accessible objects (labels for fields, etc.)

**Accessibility Tree**: Similar to the DOM in web browsers, AT-SPI2 maintains a hierarchical tree of accessible objects that mirrors your application's UI structure.

## Integration in OpenMaui

The OpenMaui.Platform.Linux implementation integrates AT-SPI2 through the `SkiaView` base class, which implements the `IAccessible` interface. This ensures that all 35+ Skia-based view implementations automatically participate in the accessibility infrastructure:

```csharp
public abstract class SkiaView : IAccessible
{
    public string? AccessibilityLabel { get; set; }
    public string? AccessibilityHint { get; set; }
    public AccessibilityRole Role { get; set; }
    public AccessibilityState State { get; private set; }
    
    // Exposed to AT-SPI2 via platform bridge
}
```

The platform automatically handles:
- **D-Bus registration**: Each window and its accessible descendants are registered with the AT-SPI2 registry
- **Event notifications**: State changes, focus events, and property updates are broadcast to listening assistive technologies
- **Query responses**: When Orca or other tools request information about UI elements, the platform provides real-time data

This architecture ensures that MAUI developers don't need to write Linux-specific accessibility code—the platform handles the AT-SPI2 integration transparently while respecting standard MAUI accessibility properties.
